title: First Law: Closed & Open Systems
summary: Energy conservation applied to thermodynamic systems. Master the closed-system balance $Q-W=\Delta U$ and the steady-flow (SSSF) balance for control volumes, the sign convention, work as the area under the $p$-$V$ curve, the four basic processes plus polytropic, and the standard flow devices (turbine, compressor, pump, nozzle, throttle, heat exchanger, mixing). Built around the real Tutorial 1 problems.
objectives: Apply the first law $Q-W=\Delta U$ to closed systems with the correct sign convention
Compute boundary work as the area under the $p$-$V$ curve for any quasi-static process
Find $W$, $Q$ and $\Delta U$ for isochoric, isobaric, isothermal, adiabatic and polytropic ideal-gas processes
Write and simplify the steady-flow energy equation for a control volume
Analyze turbines, compressors/pumps, nozzles, throttles, heat exchangers and mixing chambers
Distinguish state functions ($U$, $h$) from path functions ($Q$, $W$) and use them in cycle analysis
Compute the thermal efficiency of a closed-system cycle

blocks.text: The First Law: bookkeeping for energy

The first law of thermodynamics is just **energy conservation**: energy is never created or destroyed, only moved or converted. In a thermodynamic system, energy crosses the boundary in two ways only: as **heat** $Q$ (driven by a temperature difference) or as **work** $W$ (an organized force acting through a distance, e.g. a piston or a shaft). Whatever crosses in must show up as a change in the energy stored inside.

Everything in this topic is one master equation written for two situations: a **closed system** (fixed mass, e.g. gas in a piston-cylinder) and an **open system / control volume** (mass flows through, e.g. a turbine).

- **Closed system**: no mass crosses the boundary. The boundary may move (piston). We track $U$ (internal energy).
- **Open system (control volume)**: mass flows in and out through ports. We track enthalpy $h$ because flowing mass also carries **flow work** $pv$. We almost always analyze it at **steady state**.

blocks.caption: First law for a **closed system**. Heat in minus work out equals the rise in internal energy.

blocks.title: Sign convention (memorize this)
blocks.text:
In this course:
- $Q > 0$ when heat is **added to** the system.
- $W > 0$ when work is **done by** the system (e.g. gas expands and pushes the piston).

So $Q_{12}-W_{12}=\Delta U$. Heating raises $U$; doing work (expanding) lowers $U$. A stirrer/electric resistor does work **on** the system, so its $W$ is **negative**.

**Heat and work are path functions; energy is a state function.** $U$ depends only on the state (so $\Delta U$ is the same for any path between A and B), but $Q$ and $W$ depend on *how* you get there. That is the whole point of Exercise 1.1: $\Delta U_{AB}$ is fixed at 50 kJ no matter the path, while $Q$ and $W$ change with the path.
Work as the area under the p-V curve

For a closed system undergoing a **quasi-static** (slow, reversible) process, the boundary work is the area under the process line in the $p$-$V$ diagram. "Quasi-static" matters: only then does a single pressure $p(V)$ exist so the integral is defined (quiz Q2).

blocks.caption: Positive when the gas expands ($V_2>V_1$), negative when compressed.

blocks.title: p-V process explorer
blocks.caption: Drag the process between two states and watch the area under the curve (the work) change. Compare isobaric, isothermal, adiabatic and polytropic paths between the same endpoints.

blocks.text: The four basic closed processes (+ polytropic)
For an **ideal gas** ($pV=mRT$, $u=u(T)$, $h=h(T)$), here is the cheat sheet. Recall $\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$ **always** for an ideal gas (quiz Q5), $c_p-c_v=R$, $k=c_p/c_v$.

blocks.tex: \begin{aligned}&\textbf{Isochoric }(V=\text{const}): &W&=0,\;\; Q=\Delta U=mc_v\Delta T\\&\textbf{Isobaric }(p=\text{const}): &W&=p\,\Delta V=mR\Delta T,\;\; Q=mc_p\Delta T\\&\textbf{Isothermal }(T=\text{const}): &\Delta U&=0,\;\; Q=W=mRT\ln\tfrac{V_2}{V_1}\\&\textbf{Adiabatic }(Q=0): &W&=-\Delta U=-mc_v\Delta T,\;\; pV^k=\text{const}\end{aligned}
blocks.caption: The four standard processes. Each gives $W$, $Q$ and $\Delta U$ directly.
Polytropic work. Note: $n=0$ isobaric, $n=1$ isothermal, $n=k$ adiabatic, $n=\infty$ isochoric.

blocks.title: Adiabatic (isentropic) relations
blocks.text:
For a reversible adiabatic ideal-gas process:
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1}=\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}=\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{k-1}$$
Use the pressure form for turbines/compressors (you know inlet/outlet pressures), the volume form for piston cycles (you know the volumes).

blocks.title: Favourite professor traps
blocks.text:
- **Temperatures in Kelvin.** $T(\mathrm K)=T(^\circ\mathrm C)+273$. Any ratio $T_2/T_1$ or $p_2/p_1$ must use absolute T.
- **$c_v$ vs $c_p$.** $\Delta U=mc_v\Delta T$, $\Delta H=mc_p\Delta T$. Using $c_p$ for $\Delta U$ is the classic error.
- **Sign of W.** Compression and stirrers give $W<0$. A negative $W$ that you forgot to keep negative flips the answer.
- **Isothermal $\ne$ adiabatic.** Both look like curves on $p$-$V$; the adiabat is steeper ($pV^k$).

steps.text: Identify the system and the process type (which variable is held constant?). Draw it on a p-V sketch.
Compute the work: $W=\int p\,dV$ using the matching formula (0 if isochoric, $p\Delta V$ if isobaric, $mRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$ if isothermal, $(p_2V_2-p_1V_1)/(1-n)$ if polytropic).
Compute $\Delta U = mc_v(T_2-T_1)$ for ideal gas (or $U_2-U_1$ from tables for steam).
Get the missing quantity from $Q-W=\Delta U$.
For a cycle: $\sum Q_i=\sum W_i$ (since $\oint dU=0$), and $\eta=W_{net}/Q_{in}$.

blocks.title: Worked: isobaric heating of air (Tutorial 1.13)
blocks.text:
Air ($R=287$ J/kgK, $k=1.4$) at $p_1=1$ bar, $T_1=293$ K, $V_1=10^{-3}$ m$^3$ is heated at constant pressure to $T_2=533$ K. Find $W$ and $Q$.

- Mass: $m=\dfrac{p_1V_1}{RT_1}=\dfrac{10^5\cdot10^{-3}}{287\cdot293}=1.19\times10^{-3}$ kg.
- Work (isobaric): $W=mR\Delta T=1.19\times10^{-3}\cdot287\cdot240=82.6$ J.
- $c_v=R/(k-1)=717.5$ J/kgK, so $\Delta U=mc_v\Delta T=206.6$ J.
- First law: $Q=\Delta U+W=206.6+82.6=\mathbf{289.2\ J}$.

Check: $Q=mc_p\Delta T$ with $c_p=1004.5$ gives the same 289 J.

question.prompt: A rigid (constant-volume) tank holds an ideal gas. 40 kJ of heat is added. What is $\Delta U$?

options.content: $+40$ kJ, because $W=0$ in a rigid tank
$0$, because internal energy is a state function

question.explanation: Rigid tank means $V=$ const, so $W=\int p\,dV=0$. The first law $Q-W=\Delta U$ gives $\Delta U=Q=+40$ kJ. **B** confuses 'state function' (path-independent) with 'unchanging'. **C** has the wrong sign: $Q>0$ is heat added. **D** is wrong: the first law gives $\Delta U$ directly without needing T.
question.theory: For an isochoric process $W=0$, so all heat goes into internal energy: $\Delta U=Q$.

blocks.text: Open systems: the steady-flow energy equation

In an open system mass flows through a fixed control volume. At **steady state** nothing inside changes with time: mass in = mass out ($\dot m_{in}=\dot m_{out}=\dot m$) and the stored energy is constant ($dE_{cv}/dt=0$). The flowing fluid carries internal energy **plus** flow work $pv$, which combine into enthalpy $h=u+pv$. That is why open systems use $h$, not $u$.

blocks.tex: \dot Q_{12}-\dot W_{12}=\dot m\Big[\Delta h_{12}+\tfrac{\overline V_2^{\,2}-\overline V_1^{\,2}}{2}+g(z_2-z_1)\Big]
blocks.caption: SSSF (steady-state steady-flow) energy balance. $\overline V$ = flow speed, $z$ = elevation. Same sign convention: $\dot Q$ in +, $\dot W$ (shaft work) out +.

blocks.text:
Solving for the form that appears in the tutorials (out = state 2, in = state 1):
$$\dot W_{cv}=\dot Q_{cv}+\dot m\Big[(h_{in}-h_{out})+\tfrac{\overline V_{in}^2-\overline V_{out}^2}{2}+g(z_{in}-z_{out})\Big]$$
For a reversible flow process the shaft work per unit mass is $\dot W/\dot m=-\int_1^2 v\,dp$ (note: $-\int v\,dp$, **not** $\int p\,dv$).

blocks.title: What to drop for each device
blocks.text:
Most devices let you kill several terms:
- **Turbine / compressor / pump**: $\Delta ke\approx0$, $\Delta pe\approx0$, often adiabatic ($\dot Q=0$) $\Rightarrow \dot W/\dot m=h_1-h_2$.
- **Nozzle / diffuser**: $\dot W=0$, adiabatic $\Rightarrow h_1+\tfrac{\overline V_1^2}{2}=h_2+\tfrac{\overline V_2^2}{2}$ (enthalpy converts to kinetic energy).
- **Throttle (valve)**: $\dot W=0$, $\dot Q=0$, $\Delta ke\approx0 \Rightarrow h_1=h_2$ (**isenthalpic**).
- **Heat exchanger / mixing**: $\dot W=0$; energy balance on enthalpies of all streams.

blocks.title: Worked: adiabatic steam turbine (Tutorial 1.6)
blocks.text:
Steam enters at 50 bar, 500 $^\circ$C and leaves as saturated vapor at 5 bar. Adiabatic, $\Delta ke=\Delta pe=0$. Find $\dot W/\dot m$.

- From superheated tables: $h_1=3433.9$ kJ/kg.
- From saturation tables: $h_2=h_g(5\,\text{bar})=2748.6$ kJ/kg.
- SSSF with $\dot Q=0$, $\Delta ke=\Delta pe=0$:
$$\frac{\dot W}{\dot m}=h_1-h_2=3433.9-2748.6=\mathbf{685.3\ kJ/kg}$$
Positive: the turbine produces work, as expected.

question.prompt: Steam is throttled through a partly-open valve. Which statement is correct (adiabatic, $\Delta ke,\Delta pe\approx0$)?

options.content: $h_1=h_2$: enthalpy is conserved across the valve
$T_1=T_2$ always, because no heat is exchanged

question.explanation: A throttle has no shaft work ($\dot W=0$), is adiabatic ($\dot Q=0$) and negligible $\Delta ke/\Delta pe$, so SSSF gives $h_1=h_2$ (isenthalpic). **A** is wrong: a valve produces no work. **C** is wrong: it is enthalpy ($u+pv$), not $u$, that is conserved. **D** is wrong: temperature usually changes (Joule-Thomson effect, quiz Q6); only ideal gases keep $T$ constant since $h=h(T)$.
question.theory: Throttle: $\dot W=\dot Q=0,\ \Delta ke\approx0\Rightarrow h_1=h_2$. Real fluids change T (Joule-Thomson); ideal gases don't.

blocks.title: Worked: control volume with KE and heat (Tutorial 1.5)
blocks.text:
Liquid enters at $\dot m=4$ kg/s, $h_{in}=168$ kJ/kg, $\overline V_{in}=90$ m/s and leaves at $h_{out}=176$ kJ/kg, $\overline V_{out}=15$ m/s, same elevation. Heat added $\dot Q=27$ kW. Find the power $\dot W$.

$$\dot W=\dot Q+\dot m\Big[(h_{in}-h_{out})+\tfrac{\overline V_{in}^2-\overline V_{out}^2}{2}\Big]$$
$$=27000+4\Big[(168-176)\cdot10^3+\tfrac{90^2-15^2}{2}\Big]$$
$$=27000+4(-8000+3937.5)=\mathbf{10\,750\ W=10.75\ kW}$$
The kinetic-energy term ($+3937.5$ J/kg) is not negligible here because the speeds are high.
Mixing and heat exchangers

For a mixing chamber or heat exchanger, write the SSSF balance with **all streams** and $\dot W=0$. For liquid water with constant $c_p$, $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$. Tutorial 1.20: two water streams mix.

$$\dot Q_{cv}=\dot m_1 c_p(T_3-T_1)+\dot m_2 c_p(T_3-T_2)$$
With $\dot m_1=2$, $\dot m_2=1$ kg/s, $c_p=4.2$, $T_1=20$, $T_2=50$, $T_3=25\,^\circ$C: $\dot Q=2\cdot4.2\cdot5+1\cdot4.2\cdot(-25)=42-105=\mathbf{-63\ kW}$ (heat rejected). For **adiabatic** mixing ($\dot Q=0$) the outlet is the mass-weighted average: $T_3'=\dfrac{\dot m_1T_1+\dot m_2T_2}{\dot m_1+\dot m_2}=\dfrac{2\cdot293+1\cdot323}{3}=303$ K.

blocks.title: Closed-system cycle efficiency
blocks.text: For a thermodynamic cycle, $\Delta U_{cycle}=0$, so the net heat equals the net work: $\sum Q_i=\sum W_i=W_{net}$. Thermal efficiency is $\eta=\dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ where $Q_{in}$ is the **sum of only the positive (added) heats**. In Tutorial 1.4, $\eta=\dfrac{359.5}{1765}=0.2$; in 1.14, $\eta=\dfrac{255.8}{588.5}=0.43$ (only $Q_{23}>0$); in 1.15, $\eta=\dfrac{96.9}{234.8+53}=0.337$.

practice.prompt: A closed system receives $Q=80$ kJ of heat and does $W=30$ kJ of work on the surroundings. What is the change in internal energy?
practice.explanation: First law: $\Delta U=Q-W=80-30=+50$ kJ. **A** adds instead of subtracting the work. **C** has the wrong sign (would need $W-Q$). **D** adds with the wrong sign. The system is heated more than it does work, so $U$ rises.
practice.theory: Closed system: $Q_{12}-W_{12}=\Delta U$, with $Q$ in positive and $W$ out positive.

practice.prompt: A gas ($p_1=3$ bar, $V_1=0.1$ m$^3$) expands to $V_2=0.2$ m$^3$ at constant pressure. The work done by the gas is:
practice.explanation: Isobaric ($n=0$): $W=p\,\Delta V=3\times10^5\cdot(0.2-0.1)=30\,000$ J $=30$ kJ. **B** doubles it. **C** is the isothermal result $p_1V_1\ln(V_2/V_1)$ (wrong process). **D** would be for constant volume.
practice.theory: Isobaric work: $W=p(V_2-V_1)$. This is the $n=0$ polytropic case.

practice.prompt: The same gas instead expands **isothermally** from $V_1=0.1$ to $V_2=0.2$ m$^3$ (start $p_1=3$ bar). The work is closest to:
practice.explanation: Isothermal ($n=1$): $W=p_1V_1\ln(V_2/V_1)=3\times10^5\cdot0.1\cdot\ln 2=20.8$ kJ. **A** is the isobaric answer. **D** is the $n=1.5$ polytropic answer. **B** is just a wrong round number. The isothermal area lies between the steeper polytrope and the flat isobar.

practice.prompt: 8 kg/s of helium ($R=2077$ J/kgK) is compressed isothermally at 300 K from $v_A=35$ to $v_B=20$ m$^3$/kg. The rate of work transfer is closest to:
practice.explanation: $\dot W=\dot m R T\ln(v_B/v_A)=8\cdot2077\cdot300\cdot\ln(20/35)=-2.79$ MW. Compression means work is done **on** the gas, so $\dot W<0$. **A** has the wrong sign. **C** confuses $\Delta U=0$ (true) with $W=0$ (false; $Q=W\ne0$). **D** uses the wrong $T$ or numbers.
practice.theory: Isothermal flow/closed: $\Delta U=0$ so $Q=W=\dot m RT\ln(v_2/v_1)$. Compression to smaller $v$ gives negative work.

practice.prompt: In a piston-cylinder, an ideal gas follows $pV=$ const. The internal energy rises by 12 kJ while $p$ goes 10$\to$40 bar with $V_1=0.1$ m$^3$. The heat transfer is:
practice.explanation: $V_2=p_1V_1/p_2=0.025$ m$^3$. Isothermal work $W=p_1V_1\ln(V_2/V_1)=10^6\cdot0.1\cdot\ln(0.25)=-140$ kJ. Then $Q=\Delta U+W=12+(-140)=-128$ kJ. **A** gives $-W$ alone. **B** ignores the (compression) work. **D** has the wrong sign.
practice.theory: $pV=$const is isothermal for an ideal gas. $W<0$ on compression; $Q=\Delta U+W$.

practice.prompt: An adiabatic piston-cylinder follows a straight line on the p-V plane from (1 MPa, 1 m$^3$) to (200 kPa, 3 m$^3$). The change in internal energy is:
practice.explanation: Work = trapezoid area $=\frac{(p_1+p_2)}{2}(V_2-V_1)=\frac{1000+200}{2}\cdot2=1200$ kJ. Adiabatic: $Q=0$, so $\Delta U=Q-W=-1200$ kJ. **B** drops the minus sign. **C** forgets the factor 1/2 (treats it as a rectangle). **D** wrongly assumes adiabatic means $\Delta U=0$.
practice.theory: Straight line on p-V: $W=$ trapezoid area $=\frac12(p_1+p_2)(V_2-V_1)$. Adiabatic $\Rightarrow\Delta U=-W$.

practice.prompt: An adiabatic, rigid tank holds 40 kg of water at 30 $^\circ$C. A stirrer delivers 300 kW. With $c=4186$ J/kgK, the time to reach 36 $^\circ$C is closest to:
practice.explanation: $\Delta U=mc\Delta T=40\cdot4186\cdot6=1004$ kJ. Adiabatic, so $\Delta U=-W=-\dot W t$. The stirrer does work on the system: $\dot W=-300$ kW, hence $t=\Delta U/300=1004/300=3.35$ s. **B/C** are off by factors of 10. **D** uses a wrong $\Delta T$.
practice.theory: Stirrer work is negative (done on the system). Adiabatic closed: $\Delta U=-W=-\dot W t$.

practice.prompt: An adiabatic steam turbine takes steam at $h_1=3433.9$ kJ/kg and exhausts at $h_2=2748.6$ kJ/kg ($\Delta ke=\Delta pe\approx0$). The specific work output is:
practice.explanation: Adiabatic SSSF: $\dot W/\dot m=h_1-h_2=3433.9-2748.6=685.3$ kJ/kg, positive (turbine produces work). **B** has the wrong sign (that would be a compressor on this fluid). **C** adds the enthalpies. **D** just reports $h_2$.
practice.theory: Adiabatic turbine: $\dot W/\dot m=h_{in}-h_{out}$; positive means work produced.

practice.prompt: Air ($k=1.4$, $c_v=717.5$ J/kgK) enters an adiabatic compressor at 20 $^\circ$C, 1 bar and exits at 5 bar (reversible). The outlet temperature and specific work input are about:
practice.explanation: Isentropic: $T_2=293\cdot5^{0.4/1.4}=464$ K $=191\,^\circ$C. With $c_p=kc_v=1004.5$, $\dot W/\dot m=c_p(T_1-T_2)=1004.5(293-464)=-171.8$ kJ/kg (work **input**, negative). **B** has the wrong (positive) sign. **C** uses a wrong $T_2$. **D** confuses 464 K with 464 $^\circ$C.

practice.prompt: An ideal gas in a rigid tank at 2.5 bar, 20 $^\circ$C is heated until $p=5$ bar. The final temperature is:
practice.explanation: Rigid tank $\Rightarrow V$ const, so $p/T=$ const: $T_2=T_1(p_2/p_1)=293\cdot2=586$ K $=313\,^\circ$C. **A** wrongly doubles the Celsius value ($20\to40$). **C** confuses 586 K with 586 $^\circ$C. **D** halves wrongly. Always use Kelvin in the ratio.

practice.prompt: Air ($c_p=1004.5$ J/kgK, $k=1.4$) enters a compressor at 100 kPa, 15 $^\circ$C and leaves at 400 kPa, 350 $^\circ$C. The isentropic efficiency is closest to:
practice.explanation: $T_{2s}=288\cdot4^{0.4/1.4}=428$ K. $\eta_{is,c}=\dfrac{T_{2s}-T_1}{T_2-T_1}=\dfrac{428-288}{623-288}=0.42$. **B** is $1-\eta$. **C** ignores irreversibility. **D** inverts the ratio (using turbine form). For a compressor the ideal work is the smaller one, so $\eta_{is,c}<1$.

practice.prompt: A tank holds 1 kg of saturated water mixture at 1 bar ($h_f=417$, $h_g=2675$ kJ/kg), quality $x=0.75$. Heat added at constant pressure to reach saturated vapor is:
practice.explanation: $Q=m(h_g-h_A)=m(1-x)(h_g-h_f)=1\cdot0.25\cdot(2675-417)=564.5$ kJ. **A** is the full latent heat $h_{fg}$ (as if $x=0$). **C** uses $x=0.75$ of $h_{fg}$ (wrong fraction). **D** is unrelated. Only the remaining $(1-x)$ liquid must be vaporized.

practice.prompt: Which set of quantities are all path functions (depend on the process, not just endpoints)?
practice.explanation: Work, heat and entropy production $\sigma$ all depend on the path. **A** lists state functions ($U,h,s$). **C** lists state properties. **D** mixes a path function (work) with two state functions. State functions have exact differentials; path functions do not.
practice.theory: State functions ($U,H,S,p,V,T$) depend only on the state; $Q$, $W$, $\sigma$ depend on the path.

practice.prompt: For a closed-system thermodynamic cycle, which is always true?
practice.explanation: $U$ is a state function, so over a closed cycle $\Delta U=0$, which forces $\sum Q_i=\sum W_i=W_{net}$. **B** and **C** are false (net heat and net work are nonzero and equal for a power cycle). **D** is wrong: efficiency divides by $Q_{in}$ (sum of positive heats only), not by the sum of absolute values.
practice.theory: Cycle: $\oint dU=0\Rightarrow\sum Q=\sum W=W_{net}$; $\eta=W_{net}/Q_{in}$ with $Q_{in}$ the added heat only.

practice.prompt: In an adiabatic nozzle, steam enters slowly with $h_1=3000$ kJ/kg and leaves at $h_2=2900$ kJ/kg with negligible inlet speed. The outlet speed is closest to:
practice.explanation: Nozzle: $\dot W=0$, adiabatic, $\overline V_1\approx0$, so $h_1=h_2+\overline V_2^2/2$. Then $\overline V_2=\sqrt{2(h_1-h_2)}=\sqrt{2\cdot100\,000}=447$ m/s. **B** forgets the factor 2 and the kJ$\to$J conversion. **C** forgets the $10^3$ entirely. **D** is a wrong round value. Enthalpy drop converts to kinetic energy.
practice.theory: Adiabatic nozzle: $h_1+\tfrac{\overline V_1^2}{2}=h_2+\tfrac{\overline V_2^2}{2}$; with $\overline V_1\approx0$, $\overline V_2=\sqrt{2\Delta h}$.

exam.title: Three-process gas cycle (nitrogen)
exam.statement: A closed system ($V_1=3$ dm$^3$) contains $m=30$ g of nitrogen (ideal gas, $k=1.399$, $R=296.77$ J/kgK) at $-50\,^\circ$C. It undergoes three quasi-static processes: (1$\to$2) adiabatic expansion with $V_2=3V_1$; (2$\to$3) isothermal compression to $V_3=V_1$; (3$\to$1) constant-volume heating. Draw the cycle on a p-V diagram and find $W$, $Q$, $\Delta U$ for each process and the thermal efficiency.
exam.tips: Use Kelvin everywhere. For the adiabat use $TV^{k-1}=$const. Efficiency divides by the **positive** heat only. Sketch: 1→2 steep down-right (adiabat), 2→3 isotherm up-left, 3→1 vertical up at constant V.

exam.title: Steam turbine with kinetic and potential energy
exam.statement: Steam flows through an adiabatic turbine at 5000 kg/h producing 500 kW. (a) Find $\Delta h=h_1-h_2$ neglecting kinetic and potential energy. (b) Now include inlet speed 40 m/s, outlet speed 250 m/s and an elevation change $\Delta z=z_1-z_2=-3$ m. Find $\Delta h$.

steps.title: (a) Simplified SSSF
steps.content: $\dot W=\dot m(h_1-h_2)\Rightarrow\Delta h=\dot W/\dot m=500/1.39=359.7$ kJ/kg.

steps.title: (b) Full balance
steps.content: $\dfrac{\dot W}{\dot m}=(h_1-h_2)+\dfrac{\overline V_1^2-\overline V_2^2}{2}+g(z_1-z_2)$, so $\Delta h=\dfrac{\dot W}{\dot m}-\dfrac{\overline V_1^2-\overline V_2^2}{2}-g\Delta z$.

exam.tips: The KE term dominates the correction (210 m/s difference). The PE term ($\approx0.03$ kJ/kg) is tiny but shown for completeness. Watch the signs of the velocity and elevation differences.

exam.title: Spring-loaded piston with steam (extrapolation)
exam.statement: A cylinder ($d=20$ cm) holds $m=0.01$ kg of steam at quality $x_1=0.9$. A piston (mass 160 kg) rests on the steam; a spring ($K=50$ kN/m) just touches the top of the piston. Heat is supplied until the spring is compressed $\Delta z=15.7$ cm. Find the final temperature. Take $p_{atm}=1$ bar.

steps.title: Final temperature
steps.content: At 4 bar, $v_2=1.535$ m$^3$/kg is far into superheat (above the table range). Treat as ideal gas: $T_2=\dfrac{p_2v_2}{R}=\dfrac{4\times10^5\cdot1.535}{461.5}=1330$ K $=1057\,^\circ$C.

exam.tips: The piston weight sets the initial pressure; the spring force $K\Delta z/A$ adds on top for the final pressure. When $v$ exceeds the table range at low $p$ and high $T$, superheated steam behaves like an ideal gas.

exam.title: Mixing of two water streams
exam.meta: Tutorial 1.20 · open system / mixing · numeric + conceptual
exam.statement: Two water streams mix at constant pressure, steady state, no work. Stream 1: $\dot m_1=2$ kg/s at $20\,^\circ$C. Stream 2: $\dot m_2=1$ kg/s at $50\,^\circ$C. Take $c_p=4.2$ kJ/kgK, $\Delta ke=\Delta pe\approx0$. (a) Find the heat rate $\dot Q$ if the outlet is $25\,^\circ$C. (b) Find the adiabatic outlet temperature.
exam.given: $\dot m_3=\dot m_1+\dot m_2=3$ kg/s; $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$ for liquid water.

steps.title: Energy balance form
steps.content: With $\dot W=0$: $\dot Q_{cv}=\dot m_1(h_3-h_1)+\dot m_2(h_3-h_2)=\dot m_1c_p(T_3-T_1)+\dot m_2c_p(T_3-T_2)$.

steps.title: (a) T₃ = 25 °C
steps.content: $\dot Q=2\cdot4.2\cdot(25-20)+1\cdot4.2\cdot(25-50)=42-105=-63$ kW. Negative: heat is rejected to the surroundings to cool the mix below the natural mixing temperature.

steps.title: (b) Adiabatic mixing
steps.content: Set $\dot Q=0$: $0=\dot m_1(T_3'-T_1)+\dot m_2(T_3'-T_2)$, so $T_3'=\dfrac{\dot m_1T_1+\dot m_2T_2}{\dot m_1+\dot m_2}$.

exam.tips: Adiabatic mixing gives the mass-weighted (not arithmetic) average temperature. If the required outlet is colder than that average, heat must be removed ($\dot Q<0$).
